title: Add agent/retention.py â€” single policy engine for log rotation, table pruning, SQLite vacuum
depends_on: T-106 (DB connection discipline for SQLite ops)
blocks: T-110 (streaming readers depend on rotated archives existing)
current_state: Five data sources grow without bound: (1) logs/turns.jsonl appended forever, no rotation. (2) logs/evolution.jsonl appended forever. (3) data/watchers.db::watcher_events table never pruned (10 watchers x 1/min = 14k rows/day = 5M rows/year). (4) data/memory_replication.log appended without size limit. (5) data/pi.db (SQLite L3) never VACUUMed â€” WAL files grow larger than the main DB after months of L3 deletes/updates. After ~3-6 months of normal operation each of these starts producing real symptoms: OOM on log scans, slow watcher queries, disk pressure.
structural_problem: There is no shared retention abstraction. Each data source would need its own ad-hoc rotation, with its own bugs. A central policy engine + dataclass declaration of intent solves all five at once.
target_state: agent/retention.py exports Policy dataclass, run_policy(p), run_all(policies), and DEFAULT_POLICIES list. The five policies (jsonl_rotate, sqlite_table_prune, log_size_rotate, sqlite_vacuum) cover the five sources. State is tracked per-policy in data/retention_state.json so policies don't double-run. Entry points (T-112) hook into cron + session-end.
design_notes: Policy dataclass fields: name (str), path (str|Path), kind (Literal), max_age_days (int|None), max_size_mb (int|None), keep_archives (int|None), archive_dir (str), table (str|None), timestamp_col (str|None), vacuum_after (bool=False), schedule (Literal['daily','weekly','on_demand']='daily')

jsonl_rotate semantics: if file mtime crossed UTC midnight boundary and not already rotated today, move to {archive_dir}/{name}-{YYYY-MM-DD}.jsonl then gzip in-place; truncate the source file. Keep latest keep_archives, delete older.
sqlite_table_prune semantics: DELETE FROM {table} WHERE {timestamp_col} < datetime('now', '-{max_age_days} days'). If vacuum_after=True and rows>0 deleted, VACUUM after.
log_size_rotate semantics: if file size > max_size_mb, rename to {path}.1, {path}.1 â†’ {path}.2, etc., up to keep_archives.
sqlite_vacuum semantics: open connection, execute VACUUM, close. Track last_vacuum_at; skip if last_vacuum_at within schedule window (weekly = 7 days).
DEFAULT_POLICIES list: 5 Policy instances covering audit items #10-#14 #20
run_policy returns dict: {'name': str, 'applied': bool, 'reason': str, 'stats': {...}, 'duration_s': float}
run_all returns dict: {'policies_run': int, 'applied': int, 'errors': int, 'details': [run_policy results]}
All exceptions in run_policy caught and reported via track_silent('retention.<policy_name>', e); function returns 'applied': False, 'reason': str(e)
State file data/retention_state.json: {policy_name: {'last_run': iso_timestamp, 'last_applied': iso_timestamp, 'last_stats': {...}}} â€” read at start of run_policy, written at end

Critical: NEVER delete the source data without first rotating to archive. Crash-safety: do rename â†’ gzip â†’ truncate; if crash mid-flow, archive exists, source still readable. Atomic order matters.
success_criteria: All 4 policy kinds work end-to-end with synthetic data (covered by tests)
DEFAULT_POLICIES = 5 policies matching the 5 audit-referenced sources
After running jsonl_rotate on a 100KB file, archive exists and source file is empty
After running sqlite_table_prune with rows older than max_age, those rows are deleted; recent rows remain
ADR-006 documents the policy decisions (why 90 days for turns, 180 for evolution, 30 for watcher_events, weekly VACUUM, etc.)
risk_notes: Data loss risk: any rotation/prune that hits an edge case could delete data permanently. Mitigations: (1) archive-before-truncate ordering; (2) every policy supports a 'dry_run: bool' parameter; (3) verify-bound tests using temp dirs; (4) state file shows what was last applied so a stuck policy is obvious; (5) keep_archives keeps multiple recent copies. Concurrency risk: cron and session-end could both invoke run_all simultaneously. Mitigation: file-lock data/retention_state.json during run.
migration_plan: 1. Write agent/retention.py with Policy dataclass + run_policy() dispatch + 4 kind-specific handlers + DEFAULT_POLICIES + state file IO + file lock around state
2. Write docs/adr/006-retention-architecture.md documenting the 5 default policies and rationale (max_age choices, vacuum cadence)

3. Add testing/test_retention.py with one test per policy kind using tmp_path fixtures: synthetic jsonl that crosses midnight, synthetic sqlite table with mixed-age rows, oversized log file, sqlite db with bloat
4. Include a dry_run test for each kind to verify no destructive ops occur in that mode
5. Include a state file roundtrip test (write, read, last_run honored)
6. Include a file-lock test: two parallel run_all calls; one completes, one blocks-then-skips
7. NO wiring into runtime yet â€” that is T-112
test_plan: test_jsonl_rotate_creates_archive â€” create 100-line jsonl with mtime yesterday; run; assert .jsonl.gz exists and source empty
test_jsonl_rotate_skips_if_not_due â€” file mtime today; assert no rotation
test_jsonl_rotate_keeps_archives_count â€” run 10 days simulated; assert keep_archives N archives present, older deleted
test_sqlite_table_prune_removes_old â€” insert mixed-age rows; run; assert only recent kept
test_sqlite_table_prune_vacuum_after â€” same, with vacuum_after=True; assert db file size shrinks
test_log_size_rotate_rolls_when_oversized â€” create 60MB log, max_size_mb=50; run; assert .1 file exists
test_sqlite_vacuum_runs_weekly â€” last_vacuum_at=now; run; assert skipped. last_vacuum_at=8d ago; run; assert applied.
test_dry_run_no_destructive_ops â€” every kind: dry_run=True; assert no source file mutated
test_concurrent_run_all_uses_lock â€” two threads call run_all; assert only one runs at a time
test_policy_failure_recorded_in_track_silent â€” patch handler to raise; run_policy returns applied=False; track_silent called with 'retention.<name>'
